info.title: Inuvi Client Integration API Documentation
info.description:
The Inuvi API lets you:

  * create and get orders,
  * create and get order exams,
  * create order exam requirements
  * and more...

get.summary: Get applicant Address Types for logged OC user.

Token.description:
All provided endpoints require authentication. To become authenticated you will need to generate an **Access token** from admin panel. Once you have your **Access token**, you can pass it in as request header parameter like this: 

```
 Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN_HERE 
```
Token.name: Authorization

tags.name: Introduction
tags.description: The **Inuvi API** is built using REST principles and it has predictable resource URLs that makes writing applications easy. It returns HTTP response codes to indicate errors, it also accepts and returns JSON in the HTTP body. This API follows HTTP rules, enabling a wide range of HTTP clients can be used to interact with the API. Every resource is exposed as an URL. You can use your favorite HTTP/REST library for your programming language to use Inuvi's API.

tags.name: Authentication
tags.description:
All provided endpoints require authentication. To become authenticated you will need to generate an **Access token** from the [admin panel](https://mssportalstg.medscreensolutions.com). Once you have your **Access token**, you can pass it in an authorization request header when making API requests like this: 

```
 Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN_HERE 
```

tags.name: HTTP Methods
tags.description:
Inuvi API uses appropriate HTTP verbs for actions:    
| Method | Description |
| ------- | -------- | 
| **GET**   | Used for retrieving resources.    |
| **POST**   | Used for creating resources and performing resource actions.    |


**MIME Types**

All requests and responses are JSON-formatted and UTF-8 encoded.

```
 Accept: application/json 
```

An Accept header is required for all requests:A *Content-Type* header must be given when sending data to our API (using *POST* endpoints), for example:

```
 Content-Type: application/json 
```

tags.name: Errors
tags.description:
Our API uses HTTP status codes to indicate success or failure of an API call. Commonly used HTTP status codes are listed below. 
| Status Code | Description |
| ------- | -------- | 
| **200**   | Success - *The request was successfully completed*.    |
| **400**   | Bad request - *The request cannot be performed. Usually because of malformed parameter or missing parameter*.   |
| **500**   | Server side error - *Server encountered an error which prevents it from fulfilling the request*.    |

tags.name: API versions
tags.description:
The Inuvi API is version controlled. Versioning ensures that API REST API changes are backward compatible. By specifying a version in a REST request, you can get expected responses regardless of future changes to the API.

The version number of the REST API appears in the REST URL. Currently we only support the v1.0 version. Example request: 

```
 POST https://mssiapi.medscreensolutions.com/api/v1.0/orders 
```

tags.name: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing
tags.description: API features *Cross-Origin Resource Sharing* (CORS) implemented in compliance with [W3C spec](https://www.w3.org/TR/cors/), allow cross-domain communication from the browser. All responses have a wildcard same-origin which makes them completely public and accessible to everyone, including any code on any site.

tags.name: Application process example
tags.description:
This topic shows how to embed a Inuvi exam creation process in application.

## Create an order
To create an order, user need to call a [POST method for ORDER](/#operation/Orders_PostByorderRequest) with the request containing at least all required parameters. After sucessfull order creation process, user will receive a response containing **Id of created order**. This Id will be used in exam creation process to link order with exam.

Basic facts about order:

  * order contains common information about exams created for this order,
 * order is stateless,
 * single order can be used for multiple exams,
 * order can have multiple linked orders.

After order creation, user can receive order object using [GET order](/#operation/Orders_GetByid) method.
## Create an exam
Exam can be created using [create exam](/#operation/OrderExams_PostByorderIdorderExamRequest) method with *Order Id* as one of required parameters.
Basic facts about exam:

  * exam can be created right after order creation,
 * order has a status
 * multiple exams can exists for single order,
 * exam is assigned to examiner *(e.g. doctor, nurse ...)*.

After exam creation, user can receive exam object using [GET order](/#operation/OrderExams_GetByorderExamId) method. It is also possible to upload [AMRA file](/#operation/OrderExams_UploadAmraByorderExamId) for existing exam.

## Helpers
We also provide helper methods to make user's request easier. By using [GET linked orders](/#tag/OLinkedOrders) method you can receive all orders to be linked with another order. We also provide methods for getting

 * all possible [applicant address types](/#operation/ApplicantAddressTypes_Get),
 * [exam types](/#operation/ExamTypes_Get),
 * [life indicator values](/#operation/LifeIndicators_Get),
 * [linked order types](/#operation/LinkedOrderTypes_Get),
 * [ordering channels](/#operation/OrderingChannels_Get),
 * [order reasons for request](/#operation/OrderReasonsForRequest_Get)
 * and [types of cover](/#operation/TypesOfCover_Get).

tags.name: Webhooks
tags.description: ## What is a WebHook? The concept of a WebHook is simple. A WebHook is an HTTP callback: an HTTP POST that occurs when something happens; a simple event-notification via HTTP POST. A web application implementing WebHooks will POST a message to a URL when certain things happen. When a web application enables users to register their own URLs, the users can then extend, customize, and integrate that application with their own custom extensions or even with other applications around the web. For the user, WebHooks are a way to receive valuable information when it happens, rather than continually polling for that data and receiving nothing valuable most of the time. That URL is set up to receive the body of the POST request and process it in some way. The majority of webhooks will POST data to you in one of two ways: as JSON or XML to be interpreted, or as a form data (application/x-www-form-urlencoded or multipart/formdata). ## Securing a Webhook As webhooks deliver data to publicly available URLs in user's app, there’s the chance that someone else could find that URL and then provide you with false data. To prevent this from happening, there are a number of techniques. The easiest thing to do is to force TLS connections (https). Once you’ve implemented that, you may go forward and further secure your connection: * The first and most supported way to secure a webhook is to add tokens to the URL that act as unique identification e.g. '...?auth=TK' * The next option is to implement [Basic Auth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basic_access_authentication), this is also widely supported, and very easy to do. Basic authentication uses standard fields in the HTTP header. * The first two solutions work great to prevent most attacks, however they have the disadvantage of sending the auth token with the request. The third option is to have the provider sign each request it makes to you and then verifying the signature. The disadvantage is in requiring the provider to implement request signing. ## Inuvi's webhooks Inuvi's webhooks allow users's application to receive information about Inuvi's events as they occur. User can configure and test webhooks using the Webhooks settings page in his account. ### Add a new webhook To add a new webhook using the Inuvi web application: 1. Navigate to Webhook settings under OC level in Inuvi account. 2. Click **Add New Endpoint** at the top of the page. 3. Add webhook URL to Url field. 4. Give webhook a name to Name field. 5. Choose the events you want to listen for. 6. Click **Save**. Actions for existing webhook endpoint are **detail**, **update** and **delete**. When user provides the URL where he wants Inuvi to POST the data for events, we'll do a quick check that the URL exists by using a HEAD request (not POST). If the URL doesn't exist or returns something other than a 200 HTTP response to the HEAD request, Inuvi will fallback and attempt a POST request. In this case, the POST request, the request body will be an empty. ### Event Types | Code | Description | | ------- | -------- | | 500 | AMRA Outstanding | | 601 | New Order Creation Completed | | 603 | Assigned to Examiner | | 605 | Scheduled Exam: DD/MM/YYYY HHhMM | | 606 | Rescheduled Exam: DD/MM/YYYY HHhMM | | 610 | Completed | | 611 | Completed with Problems | | 614 | Status updated to Cancelled | | 615 | Status updated to On Hold | | 616 | Status updated to Query | | 655 | Status updated to Cancelled due to 90 days inactivity | ### Test Webhooks in Inuvi User can send test events to any webhook URL in user's Inuvi account. To test a webhook in Inuvi: 1. Navigate to Webhook settings under OC level in your Inuvi account. 2. Click **Send Test Webhook** at the top of the page. 3. If you haven't already, configure a new webhook and select the types of events you'd like to receive. 4. Choose the endpoint for test. 5. Click the **send button** to send a test event to your webhook URL. Inuvi will send one event with test data. If the POST succeeds or fails, we'll display a grid of results. Grid of results contains all results from webhook calls (test webhooks and not test webhooks). Inuvi's webhooks will POST data to you in JSON format. For example: ### Signing webhooks Inuvi signs webhook requests so user can (optionally) verify that requests are generated by Inuvi and not a third-party pretending to be Inuvi. If user's application exposes sensitive data, he may wants to be sure the requests are coming from Inuvi. This isn't required, but offers an additional layer of verification. Inuvi includes an additional HTTP header with webhook POST requests, *Webhook-Signature*, which will contain the signature for the request. To verify a webhook request, generate a signature using the same key that Inuvi uses and compare that to the value of the *Webhook-Signature* header. When user creates a webhook, a key is automatically generated. User can also view the key from the Webhook detail page in his account, in the Key column. In code, check that the webhook has a valid signature before processing it: 1. Create a string with the webhook's request body (receive as json format). 2. Encode all the characters in the specified string into a sequence of bytes (UTF-8 format). 3. Hash the resulting a sequence of bytes with HMAC-SHA256, using webhook's authentication key to generate a binary signature. 4. Compare the binary signature that were generated to the signature provided in the *Webhook-Signature* HTTP header. **Example C# implementation**
